draft.introduction: The Parallel Lives of Plutarch (c. 100 CE) pairs Greek and Roman statesmen to illuminate character through comparison. The opening Life of Theseus reveals Plutarch’s method: using biography as moral philosophy, examining virtue and vice through concrete examples.

translation.text: Just as in geographies, the historians crowd the parts of the earth that escape their knowledge into the margins of their maps, adding notes such as “beyond this lie sandy deserts full of wild beasts” or “trackless marsh” or “Scythian cold” or “frozen sea,” so in this work of mine, in which I have compared the lives of the greatest men, after passing through the periods accessible to reasonable inquiry, I might say of the remoter ages: beyond this lie the regions of prodigy and fiction.
translation.original_text: ὥσπερ ἐν ταῖς γεωγραφίαις οἱ ἱστορικοί

translator_notes.note: The geographical metaphor—pushing legendary material to the margins like uncharted territory on a map—reveals Plutarch’s sophisticated historical awareness. He does not reject myth outright but locates it at the boundary of reliable knowledge. His Lives became the most widely read classical text in Renaissance Europe, directly inspiring Shakespeare.
